name: python-oxmsg
documentation:
[![MIT License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-orange.svg)](LICENSE.txt)
[![on PyPI](https://img.shields.io/badge/pypi-0.0.1-blue.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/python-oxmsg/0.0.1/)
[![Python 3.9+](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.9+-brightgreen.svg)](https://www.python.org/downloads/)

# python-oxmsg

Parse Outlook MSG (.msg) files to extract email messages and attachments.

The target use case is extracting Outlook message text and accessing attachments. There is no support for modifying messages or creating them from scratch. In addition to message text, other message properties such as sent-date, etc. are also accessible.


## Documentation

Documentation for this project is on [GitHub Pages](https://scanny.github.io/python-oxmsg).


## Installation
```bash
pip install python-oxmsg
```
Occasionally it can be useful to install from a GitHub branch, perhaps to try out a version that has not yet been released. This command would install from the `develop` branch:
```bash
pip install git+https://github.com/scanny/python-oxmsg@develop
```

## Usage
```python
>>> from oxmsg import Message
>>> msg = Message.load("message.msg")
>>> msg.message_class
'IPM.Note'
>>> msg.attachment_count
1
>>> attachment = msg.attachments[0]
>>> attachment.attached_by_value  # -- attachment bytes only available when True --
True
>>> attachment.filename
'q1-objectives.pptx'
>>> attachment.mime_type
'application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.presentationml.presentation'
>>> attachment.size
96407
>>> attachment.last_modified.isoformat()
'2023-11-18T16:08:17+00:00'
>>> with open(attachment.filename, "wb") as f:
...     f.write(attachment.file_bytes)
```

## CLI

`python-oxmsg` includes a CLI that may be useful for diagnostic purposes.
```bash
$ oxmsg
Usage: oxmsg [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]...

  Utility CLI for `python-oxmsg`.

  Provides the subcommands listed below, useful for exploratory or diagnostic
  purposes.

Options:
  --help  Show this message and exit.

Commands:
  dump     Write a summary of the MSG file's properties to stdout.
  storage  Summarize low-level "directories and files" structure of MSG...

```

The `dump` sub-command displays all properties available in the message along with the
PID and PTYP information required to access those properties from a `Properties` object.

```bash
$ oxmsg dump message.msg

------------------
Message Properties
------------------

header-properties
-----------------
recipient_count:    1

distinguished-properties
------------------------
attachment_count:         0
internet_code_page:       utf-8
message_class:            IPM.Note
sender:                   from@domain.com
...

other properties
-----------------------------------------+---------------+--------------------
property-id                              | type          | value
-----------------------------------------+---------------+--------------------
0x0017 - PidTagImportance                | PtypInteger32 | 00 00 00 01
0x001A - PidTagMessageClass              | PtypString8   | 'IPM.Note'
0x0036 - PidTagSensitivity               | PtypInteger32 | 00 00 00 00
0x0037 - PidTagSubject                   | PtypString8   | 'A test message'
0x003B - PidTagSentRepresentingSearchKey | PtypBinary    | 21 bytes
0x003D - PidTagSubjectPrefix             | PtypString8   | ''
0x0042 - PidTagSentRepresentingName      | PtypString8   | 'from@domain.com'
...
```

## Changelog

The release history including a chronicle of notable changes with each release is
recorded in [CHANGELOG.md](https://github.com/scanny/python-oxmsg/blob/master/CHANGELOG.md).

modules.name: oxmsg.attachment
documentation:
Provides access to the properties of an attachment in an Outlook MSG file.

These properies include the attached file's original file-name, its last-modified date, size, and
content-type (MIME-type).

python
\>>> from oxmsg import Message

````pycon
>>> msg = Message.load("message.msg")
>>> msg.attachment_count
1
>>> attachment = msg.attachments[0]
>>> attachment.file_name
'financial-forecast.xlsx'
>>> with open(attachment.file_name, "wb") as f:
...     f.write()
```
````

classes.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.attached_by_value
documentation:
True when the `PidTagAttachDataBinary` property contains the attachment data.

This is as opposed to "by-reference" where only a path or URL is stored.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.file_bytes
documentation: The attachment binary, suitable for saving to a file when detaching.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.file_name
documentation:
The full name of this file as it was originally attached.

Like "FY24-quarterly-projections.xlsx". Does not include a path.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.last_modified
documentation:
Timezone-aware UTC datetime when this attachment was last modified.

`None` if this property is not present on the attachment.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.mime_type
documentation: ISO 8601 str representation of time this attachment was last modified.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.properties
documentation: Provides access to the properties of this OXMSG object.

methods.name: oxmsg.attachment.Attachment.size
documentation: Length in bytes of this attachment.
A file attached to an Outlook email message.

modules.name: oxmsg.domain.encodings
documentation:
Mapping of Microsoft code-page-identifiers (ints) to Python character-encoding names.

Derived from the table at:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/intl/code-page-identifiers

Microsoft-supported code pages that do not have a built-in Python codec are indicated by the empty
string. This allows a `.get()` on this mapping to distinguish between a codec that is unknown and
one that is recognized but not supported.

functions.name: oxmsg.domain.encodings.encoding_from_codepage
documentation:
Map `codepage` to Python character encoding like "iso-2022-jp".

- Raises `UnrecognizedCodePageError` when `codepage` is not a known Microsoft character
  codepage.
- Raises `UnsupportedEncodingError` when `codepage` is recognized but Python has no builtin
  codec for that encoding.

modules.name: oxmsg.domain.model
documentation:
Domain-model objects.

This module should have no dependencies outside the domain model. In particular, any domain model
object should be importable anywhere in the package without risk of producing an import cycle.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.UnrecognizedCodePageError
documentation: The specified code-page is not a known Microsoft encoding.
EXCEPTIONS

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.UnsupportedEncodingError
documentation: The encoding used for this MSG object has no built-in Python codec.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropStorageT

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropStorageT.properties_stream_bytes
documentation:
Bytes of stream containing properties for the top-level object this storage represents.

Every storage must have exactly one such stream.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropStorageT.property_stream_bytes
documentation: The bytes of the stream for variable-length property identified by `pid`.
The interface required of a storage object to extract the properties of that storage.
INTERFACES

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT.name
documentation: The Microsft name for this property, like "PidTagMessageClass".

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT.pid
documentation: The property-id (PID) for this property, like 0x3701 for attachment bytes.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT.ptyp
documentation: The property-type (PTYP) for this property, like 0x0102 for PtypBinary.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT.ptyp_name
documentation: The Microsft name for the type of this property, like "PtypString".

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.PropertyT.value

returns.name: bool | bytes | dt.datetime | float | int | str | uuid.UUID

documentation: The value of this property, its type depending on the property.
The interface required of a property object, regardless of its type.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.iter_attachment_storages
documentation: Generate each storage object specific to an attachment to this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.iter_recipient_storages
documentation: Generate each storage object specific to a recipient of this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.name
documentation:
The last segment of the storage path.

This is the empty string for the root storage. Other storages are named is clearly specified
ways depending on the type of top-level object they contain, e.g. attachment, recipient,
etc.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.path
documentation:
String identifier for this storage; its location within the OXMSG CFB.

- Suitable as a unique storage identifier within the MSG file.
- The path of the root storage is the empty string ("").

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.properties_stream_bytes
documentation:
Bytes of stream containing properties for the top-level object this storage represents.

Every storage must have exactly one such stream.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StorageT.property_stream_bytes
documentation: The bytes of the stream for variable-length property identified by `pid`.
Interface for a storage object.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StreamT

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StreamT.name
documentation:
The last segment of the stream path.

Each stream contains the bytes for a property. The form of a stream name is clearly
specified in the OXMSG standard and includes both the property-id (PID) and property
data-type (PTYP) for the property data it contains.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StreamT.path
documentation:
String identifier for this storage; its location within the OXMSG CFB.

Suitable as a unique storage identifier within the MSG file.

methods.name: oxmsg.domain.model.StreamT.bytes_
documentation: The bytes contained in this stream.
Interface for a stream object.

modules.name: oxmsg.domain.constants
documentation: Constant definitions, mostly property ids (PIDs) and property types (PTYPs).

modules.name: oxmsg.properties
documentation:
Provides access to all properties of a top-level object (message, recipient, attachment).

This object is accessed using the `.properties` property of a top-level object. The properties for
each top-level object are segregated into its own properties object.

Many property-ids (PIDs) and property-types (PTYPs) are available as constants in
`oxml.domain.contants`.

python
\>>> from oxmsg import Message
\>>> from oxmsg.domain import constants as c

````pycon
>>> msg = Message.load("message.msg")
>>> properties = msg.properties
>>> properties.str_prop_value(c.PID_MESSAGE_CLASS).value
'IPM.Note'
```
````

functions.name: oxmsg.properties._batched_bytes
documentation:
Batch bytes from `block` into segments of `n` bytes each.

Last batch is shorter than `n` when `block` is not evenly divisible by `n`.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.binary_prop_value
documentation:
Retrieve bytes of PtypBinary property identified by `pid`.

Returns `None` if property is not present in this collection.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.body_encoding
documentation:
The encoding used for a PidTagBody or PidTagHtml property of PtypString8/Binary.

Must be cherry-picked because it is required before constructing the properties collection.

Note when these are PtypString they unconditionally use UTF-16LE.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.date_prop_value
documentation:
Read datetime property value from the properties stream.

- Microseconds are truncated.
- Returns `None` when no `pid` property is present in properties stream.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.int_prop_value
documentation:
Retrieve int value of PtypInteger32 property identified by `pid`.

Returns `None` if no `pid` property is present in this collection.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.str_prop_value
documentation:
Retrieve str value of PtypString or PtypString8 property identified by `pid`.

Returns the empty str if property is not present in this collection.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties.string_props_are_unicode
documentation: True indicates PtypString properties in this message are encoded "utf-16-le".

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._cherry_pick_int_prop
documentation:
Get an Int32 property without triggering broader property load.

Used to solve chicken-and-egg problem of determining encoding required by string
properties before atomically loading all properties.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._get_property
documentation:
Retrieve the first property with `pid` and one of `ptyps`.

The general expectation is that at most one property with `pid` and one of `ptyps` will be
present in the collection. In the unusual case there could be more than one this method
may need to be called once for each possible type to get them all or in a particular order
of preference.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._str_prop_encoding
documentation:
The encoding used for non-body properties of PtypString8.

Must be cherry-picked because it is required before constructing the properties collection.

Note when PtypString properties are unconditionally encoded with UTF-16LE.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._prop_segment_sequence
documentation: 16-byte segments comprising property blocks from the attachment properties stream.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._properties_by_pid
documentation:
Properties in this collection grouped by property-id (PID).

Not sure if this solves an actual problem in practice, but it's at least
theoretically possible that the same PID could appear twice in a property collection
with different PTYPs.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._properties_mapping
documentation: The property objects in this collection keyed by pid.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Properties._property_sequence
documentation:
Property object for each property in this collection.

Properties are in property-id (PID) order.
Provides access to properties from an OXMSG storage.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty.factory
documentation: Construct a property object of the appropriate sub-type for `segment`.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty.name
documentation: The Microsft name for this property, like "PidTagMessageClass".

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty.pid
documentation: The property-id (PID) for this property, like 0x3701 for attachment bytes.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty.ptyp
documentation: The property-type (PTYP) for this property, like 0x0102 for PtypBinary.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty.ptyp_name
documentation: The Microsft name for the type of this property, like "PtypString".

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BaseProperty._payload
documentation: The latter 8 bytes of the property segment, where the property value is stored.
Base class for properties, providing common behaviors.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.BinaryProperty

documentation: The bytes of this binary property.
Property for PtypBinary OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.BooleanProperty

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.BooleanProperty.value
documentation: The boolean value of this property.
Property for PtypBoolean OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.Float64Property

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Float64Property.value
documentation: The 64-bit floating-point value of this property.
Property for PtypFloating64 OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.GuidProperty

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.GuidProperty.__str__
documentation: Hex str representation of this UUID like '9d947746-9662-40a8-a526-abd4faec9737'.

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.GuidProperty.value
documentation:
The value of this property as a uuid.UUID object.

The `str` value of this object is the standard-form string for the UUID, like:
'9d947746-9662-40a8-a526-abd4faec9737'.
Property for PtypGuid OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.Int16Property

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Int16Property.value
documentation: The integer value of this property.
Property for PtypInteger16 OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.Int32Property

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.Int32Property.value
documentation: The integer value of this property.
Property for PtypInteger32 OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.StringProperty

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.StringProperty.value
documentation: The decoded str from this string property.
Property for PtypString OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.String8Property

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.String8Property.value
documentation:
The encoded bytes of this string property.

The caller is responsible for determining the encoding and applying it to get a str value.
Property for PtypString8 (8-bit characters, not Unicode) OLE properties.

classes.name: oxmsg.properties.TimeProperty

methods.name: oxmsg.properties.TimeProperty.value
documentation: The value of this property as a timezone-aware `datetime`.
Property for PtypTime OLE properties.

modules.name: oxmsg.message
documentation:
The `Message` object is the primary interface object in `python-oxmsg`.

This object is returned by the `Message.load()` classmethod:

python
\>>> from oxmsg import Message

````pycon
>>> msg = Message.load("message.msg")
>>> msg.subject
'Agenda for the Acme meeting'
```
````

classes.name: oxmsg.message.Message

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.load
documentation:
Load an instance from `msg_file`.

`msg_file` can be a file-path, a file-like object, or the bytes of the MSG file.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.attachment_count
documentation: Number of attachments on this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.attachments
documentation: Attachment object for each attachment in this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.body
documentation: The plain-text body of this message; `None` if not present.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.html_body
documentation: The HTML body of this message if present, `None` otherwise.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.message_headers
documentation: From, To, Content-Type, etc. headers for this message as {name: value} mapping.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.message_class
documentation: Outlook Message Class identifier like "IPM.Note" for this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.properties
documentation: Provides access to the properties of this OXMSG object.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.recipients
documentation: `Recipient` object for each recipient of this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.sender
documentation:
Name and email address of the message sender.

Like '"John Doe" <john@bigisp.com>'.

None if it is not recorded in the message. This may occur when the message is a draft or
system-generated or perhaps in other cases.

Note that the value of the From: message header is returned when present (usually I expect)
and may contain multiple addresses separated by commas.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.sent_date
documentation:
When this message was submitted by the sender.

This value will be `None` if the message has not been sent and possibly in other cases.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message.subject
documentation: Subject line of this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message._header_prop_values
documentation:
The property values in the MSG-root properties header.

It is a tuple of the four int values:
 - next_recipient_id
 - next_attachment_id
 - recipient_count
 - attachment_count

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message._iter_attachments
documentation:
Generate `Attachment` object for each attachment in this message.

Should need to be called at most once.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message._iter_recipients
documentation:
Generate `Recipient` object for each recipient in this message.

Should need to be called at most once.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message._message_headers
documentation:
From, To, Content-Type, etc. headers for this message as Message object.

This provides case-insensitive access along with some other convenient behaviors not
available from a `dict`.

methods.name: oxmsg.message.Message._validate
documentation: Raise if this message is invalid for one of a variety of possible reasons.
An Outlook email message loaded from an OXMSG format (.msg) file.

modules.name: oxmsg.util
documentation: Stand-alone utility functions independent of OXMSG domain.

classes.name: oxmsg.util.lazyproperty

methods.name: oxmsg.util.lazyproperty.__init__
documentation:
*fget* is the decorated method (a "getter" function).

A lazyproperty is read-only, so there is only an *fget* function (a regular
@property can also have an fset and fdel function). This name was chosen for
consistency with Python's `property` class which uses this name for the
corresponding parameter.

methods.name: oxmsg.util.lazyproperty.__get__
documentation:
Called on each access of 'fget' attribute on class or instance.

*self* is this instance of a lazyproperty descriptor "wrapping" the property
method it decorates (`fget`, nominally).

*obj* is the "host" object instance when the attribute is accessed from an
object instance, e.g. `obj = Obj(); obj.fget`. *obj* is None when accessed on
the class, e.g. `Obj.fget`.

*type* is the class hosting the decorated getter method (`fget`) on both class
and instance attribute access.

methods.name: oxmsg.util.lazyproperty.__set__
documentation:
Raises unconditionally, to preserve read-only behavior.

This decorator is intended to implement immutable (and idempotent) object
attributes. For that reason, assignment to this property must be explicitly
prevented.

If this __set__ method was not present, this descriptor would become a
*non-data descriptor*. That would be nice because the cached value would be
accessed directly once set (__dict__ attrs have precedence over non-data
descriptors on instance attribute lookup). The problem is, there would be
nothing to stop assignment to the cached value, which would overwrite the result
of `fget()` and break both the immutability and idempotence guarantees of this
decorator.

The performance with this __set__() method in place was roughly 0.4 usec per
access when measured on a 2.8GHz development machine; so quite snappy and
probably not a rich target for optimization efforts.

Decorator like @property, but evaluated only on first access.

Like @property, this can only be used to decorate methods having only a `self` parameter, and
is accessed like an attribute on an instance, i.e. trailing parentheses are not used. Unlike
@property, the decorated method is only evaluated on first access; the resulting value is
cached and that same value returned on second and later access without re-evaluation of the
method.

Like @property, this class produces a *data descriptor* object, which is stored in the \_\_dict\_\_
of the *class* under the name of the decorated method ('fget' nominally). The cached value is
stored in the \_\_dict\_\_ of the *instance* under that same name.

Because it is a data descriptor (as opposed to a *non-data descriptor*), its `__get__()` method
is executed on each access of the decorated attribute; the \_\_dict\_\_ item of the same name is
"shadowed" by the descriptor.

While this may represent a performance improvement over a property, its greater benefit may be
its other characteristics. One common use is to construct collaborator objects, removing that
"real work" from the constructor, while still only executing once. It also de-couples client
code from any sequencing considerations; if it's accessed from more than one location, it's
assured it will be ready whenever needed.

Loosely based on: <https://stackoverflow.com/a/6849299/1902513>.

A lazyproperty is read-only. There is no counterpart to the optional "setter" (or deleter)
behavior of an @property. This is critically important to maintaining its immutability and
idempotence guarantees. Attempting to assign to a lazyproperty raises AttributeError
unconditionally.

The parameter names in the methods below correspond to this usage example:

```
class Obj(object)

    @lazyproperty
    def fget(self):
        return 'some result'

obj = Obj()
```

Not suitable for wrapping a function (as opposed to a method) because it is not callable.

modules.name: oxmsg.recipient
documentation:
The `Recipient` object provides access to properties for a recipient of the message.

This object is accessed using the `Message.recipients` property:
python
\>>> from oxmsg import Message

````pycon
>>> msg = Message.load("message.msg")
>>> recipient = msg.recipients[0]
>>> recipient.name
'Jane Doe'
```
````

classes.name: oxmsg.recipient.Recipient

methods.name: oxmsg.recipient.Recipient.email_address
documentation: The email address of this recipient.

methods.name: oxmsg.recipient.Recipient.name
documentation: The name of this recipient.

methods.name: oxmsg.recipient.Recipient.properties
documentation: Provides access to the properties of this OXMSG object.
A recipient of an Outlook email message.

modules.name: oxmsg.storage
documentation:
A "folder" in Microsoft Compound File Binary (CFB) format.

The CFB/OLE file format encloses a filesystem, to a first appoximation, much like a Zip
archive does. In this format, a "storage" corresponds to a directory and a "stream"
corresponds to a file. A storage can contain both streams and other storages.

Each MSG file has a "root" storage, represented in this package by `MessageStorage`.
Each attachment and recipient has their own storage in the root, with a pre-defined
name, and there are other top-level objects than can appear in a MSG file that get their
own storage.

classes.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.from_ole
documentation: Return a Storage loaded from `node` and containing its streams and sub-storages.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.iter_attachment_storages
documentation: Generate storage object specific to each attachment in this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.iter_recipient_storages
documentation: Generate storage object specific to each recipent in this message.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.name
documentation: The "directory-name" of this storage, with no path-prefix.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.properties_stream_bytes
documentation: The bytes of the one-and-only-one properties stream in this storage.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage.property_stream_bytes
documentation: Read variable-length property bytes from the stream it's stored in.

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Storage._streams_by_name
documentation: dict semantics on streams of this storage.
Container for streams and sub-storages.

classes.name: oxmsg.storage.Stream

methods.name: oxmsg.storage.Stream.name
documentation: The "filename" of this stream, with no path-prefix.
Bytes of a property of a top-level object in an OXMSG file.

modules.name: oxmsg.cli
documentation:
The command-line interface (CLI) for `python-oxmsg`.

The CLI provides the command `oxmsg`.
pyright: reportPrivateUsage=false

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.oxmsg
documentation:
Utility CLI for `python-oxmsg`.

Provides the subcommands listed below, useful for exploratory or diagnostic purposes.
TODO: add `body` sub-command
TODO: add `detach` sub-command

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.dump
documentation: Write a summary of the MSG file's properties to stdout.

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.storage
documentation: Summarize low-level "directories and files" structure of MSG.

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.dump_message_properties
documentation: A summary of this MS-OXMSG object's top-level properties.

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.dump_attachment_properties
documentation: Report of message properies suitable for writing to the console.

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.dump_recipient_properties
documentation: Report of message properies suitable for writing to the console.

functions.name: oxmsg.cli.dump_properties
documentation: A summary of these properties suitable for printing to the console.

modules.name: oxmsg.domain.reference
documentation:
Provides a catalog of OXMSG properties and property types.

This module provides mappings from integer PID and PTYP keys to meaningful names that can also be
used to find more detailed information on web-search.

In addition it is a source for property names the user can select from to reference the message
attibutes they are interested in.

Also used for discovery utilities to characterize a MSG file and its properties.

variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.property_descriptors
documentation: Mapping of property-type codes to descriptor objects.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyDescriptor

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyDescriptor.pid
documentation: The property-id. This is a 16-bit integer value specified by the MS-OXMSG standard.

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyDescriptor.ms_name
documentation:
The name like "PidTagLastModificationTime" used by Microsoft for this property.

This is the value to search on for the specification of the property.

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyDescriptor.ptyp
documentation: The property type, one of the OXMSG property types.

Describes an OXMSG property.

All attributes of an OXMSG object are properties, even though the body of variable-length
properties like PID_ATTACH_FILENAME are stored in a stream.

classes.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyTypeDescriptor

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyTypeDescriptor.ptyp
documentation: The property-type id, a 16-bit integer value specified by the MS-OXMSG standard.

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyTypeDescriptor.ms_name
documentation: The name like "PtypInteger32" used by Microsoft for this type.

class_variables.name: oxmsg.domain.reference.PropertyTypeDescriptor.python_type
documentation: The Python type a property of this OXMSG type is converted to.
Describes an OXMSG property data type.
